tasks.prompt:
Warmup — Spot the 'build once, promote unchanged' principle.

Below is a broken workflow snippet. The engineer wants the same artifact that was built in the `build` job to be deployed in `deploy-staging`, but instead they compile the app again inside the deploy job.

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build artifact
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run build
          tar -czf app.tar.gz dist
      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle
          path: app.tar.gz

  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Rebuild and deploy
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run build
          tar -czf app.tar.gz dist
          scp app.tar.gz deploy@staging.example.com:/var/www/app/
```

Task: Identify exactly what is wrong and rewrite the `deploy-staging` job so it downloads and deploys the artifact that was already built by the `build` job.
tasks.starter:
# Copy the broken deploy-staging job here and fix it.

  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # Your fix goes here
tasks.solution:
deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Download artifact
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle

      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          scp app.tar.gz deploy@staging.example.com:/var/www/app/
          ssh deploy@staging.example.com 'cd /var/www/app && tar -xzf app.tar.gz && systemctl restart app'
tasks.explanation:
The broken version runs `npm ci && npm run build` again inside the deploy job. This violates the 'build once, promote unchanged' principle: a second build could produce a subtly different bundle (different timestamp, different resolved dependency version if the registry changed, etc.), so what ships to staging may not be byte-for-byte identical to what CI tested.

The fix removes all build commands from the deploy job and replaces the checkout+build block with a single `actions/download-artifact@v4` step that pulls `app-bundle` — the exact tarball the `build` job uploaded. The job then ships that file without rebuilding anything. This guarantees what staging receives is precisely what the test suite approved.
hints: Each GitHub Actions job runs on a completely fresh virtual machine. Files created in the `build` job do not automatically appear in later jobs — you need an explicit mechanism to transfer them.
Look at what `actions/upload-artifact` does in the `build` job: it stores `app.tar.gz` under the name `app-bundle`. There is a paired action that retrieves that stored file by the same name.
Once you download the artifact, you should have `app.tar.gz` in the working directory. The `scp` and `ssh` commands from the lecture's example can then be used as-is — no `npm` commands needed.

tasks.prompt:
Core Task 1 — Build the full artifact → staging → verify pipeline.

Write a complete `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` that implements the following three-job pipeline:

1. **`build`** job:
   - Checks out the repository.
   - Simulates a build by creating a file `dist/index.html` with the content `Hello from CD pipeline`.
   - Packages `dist/` into `app.tar.gz`.
   - Uploads `app.tar.gz` as artifact name `app-bundle`.

2. **`deploy-staging`** job (runs after `build`):
   - Downloads `app-bundle`.
   - Simulates a staging deploy by extracting the tarball to `./staging-site/` and printing the content of `staging-site/dist/index.html`.
   - Runs a smoke test: checks that the extracted file contains the text `Hello from CD pipeline` (use `grep`). The job must fail if the text is not found.

The workflow should trigger on every push to the `main` branch.

No real servers or SSH keys are needed — simulate everything with shell commands.
tasks.starter:
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # TODO: checkout, create dist/index.html, tar it, upload artifact

  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # TODO: download artifact, extract, print content, smoke-test with grep
tasks.solution:
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build artifact
        run: |
          mkdir -p dist
          echo 'Hello from CD pipeline' > dist/index.html
          tar -czf app.tar.gz dist

      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle
          path: app.tar.gz

  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Download artifact
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle

      - name: Deploy to staging (simulated)
        run: |
          mkdir -p staging-site
          tar -xzf app.tar.gz -C staging-site
          echo "--- Deployed file content ---"
          cat staging-site/dist/index.html

      - name: Smoke test
        run: |
          grep -q 'Hello from CD pipeline' staging-site/dist/index.html \
            && echo 'Smoke test PASSED: expected content found.' \
            || (echo 'Smoke test FAILED: expected content missing.' && exit 1)
tasks.explanation:
Key design decisions in this solution:

- **Artifact created in `build` only.** The `dist/` folder and `app.tar.gz` are built exactly once. The deploy job never repeats this work.
- **`needs: build`** on `deploy-staging` creates an explicit dependency. GitHub Actions will not start that job until `build` has completed successfully, enforcing the gated-stage principle.
- **`upload-artifact` / `download-artifact` pairing** is how the same file crosses the job boundary. Both steps reference the identical name `app-bundle`. If the names do not match, the download fails with a 'not found' error.
- **`-C staging-site`** in `tar -xzf` extracts into a subdirectory, preventing the tarball from overwriting files in the current working directory.
- **Smoke test with `grep -q` + `exit 1`** mirrors a real health-check pattern: assert something expected is present; fail the job (and therefore the pipeline) if it is not. Using `--fail` on `curl` in a real pipeline achieves the same effect against an HTTP endpoint.
hints: The `build` job must complete all three steps in order: create the file, package it with `tar -czf`, then upload with `actions/upload-artifact@v4`. If the upload step is missing, the artifact store is empty and the download in the next job will fail.

In `deploy-staging`, the `actions/download-artifact@v4` step places `app.tar.gz` in the current working directory (the root of the runner workspace). So `tar -xzf app.tar.gz` works without specifying a path — just optionally add `-C <dir>` to extract into a subdirectory.

For the smoke test, `grep -q 'text' file` returns exit code 0 if the text is found and 1 if not. You can chain it with `|| exit 1` or wrap it in an `if` statement. The important thing is that a non-zero exit code from any `run` step marks the job as failed and stops the pipeline.

tasks.prompt:
Core Task 2 — Add a production promotion job with an environment approval gate.

Extend the workflow from Core Task 1 with a third job: **`deploy-production`**.

Requirements:
1. `deploy-production` must run only after `deploy-staging` succeeds.
2. It must use a GitHub **environment** named `production` — this is where you configure a required reviewer in real projects, giving you the Continuous Delivery gate.
3. It must download the **same** `app-bundle` artifact (do NOT rebuild).
4. Simulate the production deploy by extracting the tarball to `./prod-site/` and printing the content of `prod-site/dist/index.html`.
5. Add a final step that prints: `Promotion complete. Artifact promoted from staging to production without rebuild.`

Write only the `deploy-production` job block (you can assume the `build` and `deploy-staging` jobs from Core Task 1 are already in the file).
tasks.starter:
deploy-production:
    # TODO: set dependency, environment, download artifact, simulate deploy, print confirmation
tasks.solution:
deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production
    steps:
      - name: Download artifact
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle

      - name: Promote to production (simulated)
        run: |
          mkdir -p prod-site
          tar -xzf app.tar.gz -C prod-site
          echo "--- Production file content ---"
          cat prod-site/dist/index.html

      - name: Confirm promotion
        run: |
          echo 'Promotion complete. Artifact promoted from staging to production without rebuild.'
tasks.explanation:
Three elements make this job correct:

1. **`needs: deploy-staging`** — the production job only runs after the staging job (which includes the smoke test) has finished successfully. This is the pipeline's sequential gate: a failed smoke test stops the pipeline before it ever touches production.

2. **`environment: name: production`** — this single declaration is what turns the workflow into Continuous Delivery rather than Continuous Deployment. In GitHub, you navigate to Settings → Environments → production and add a 'Required reviewers' rule. When the pipeline reaches this job, GitHub pauses it and emails the reviewers. Only after a reviewer clicks 'Approve' does the job actually execute. Remove the approval rule, and it becomes Continuous Deployment: no gate, ships automatically.

3. **No rebuild, same artifact name** — the `download-artifact` step uses the identical `app-bundle` name. This guarantees production receives the same bytes that were built in the `build` job and verified on staging. If you renamed it or omitted the step, you would have to rebuild, breaking the immutable-artifact guarantee.
hints: The `needs` field accepts either a single job name (as a string) or a list of job names. Here only one dependency is needed: the deploy-staging job. Make sure the string matches the job key exactly (including any hyphens).

The `environment` block goes at the job level, not inside `steps`. Its minimal form is just `name: production`. GitHub will look up a repository environment with that exact name and enforce any protection rules (like required reviewers) you have configured there.

You do not need any special secret or token to download an artifact that was uploaded earlier in the same workflow run. `actions/download-artifact@v4` with the matching `name` is all that is required.

tasks.prompt:
Applied Task — Design a Continuous Delivery vs. Continuous Deployment toggle and explain the trade-offs.

You are advising a team that currently uses a fully manual deploy process (a human runs a script on Fridays). They want to adopt GitHub Actions CD. Two engineers disagree:

- **Engineer A** says: 'We should go straight to Continuous Deployment — if the tests pass, ship it automatically, no approvals.'
- **Engineer B** says: 'We should start with Continuous Delivery — keep a human approval gate on the production environment for now.'

Complete both parts:

**Part A (code):** Take the three-job workflow you built in Core Tasks 1 and 2 and show the single YAML change that switches it from Continuous Delivery (human gate) to Continuous Deployment (no gate). Explain in one sentence why that change alone is sufficient.

**Part B (written):** Write 3–5 sentences explaining which approach you would recommend for this team *right now*, and what conditions would need to be true before they could safely remove the approval gate. Reference at least one concept from the lecture (e.g., test coverage, lead time, feature toggles, rollback speed).
tasks.starter:
# Part A: show the diff — what do you remove or change in deploy-production?
# (Write the before and after YAML blocks, then your one-sentence explanation.)

# Part B: write your recommendation below as a comment block.
# ---
tasks.solution:
# Part A

# BEFORE (Continuous Delivery — human gate in place):
  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production      # <-- this environment has 'Required reviewers' configured
    steps:
      - name: Download artifact
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle
      - name: Promote to production (simulated)
        run: |
          mkdir -p prod-site
          tar -xzf app.tar.gz -C prod-site
          cat prod-site/dist/index.html

# AFTER (Continuous Deployment — no gate):
  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # environment block removed entirely (or kept with no required-reviewers rule)
    steps:
      - name: Download artifact
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: app-bundle
      - name: Promote to production (simulated)
        run: |
          mkdir -p prod-site
          tar -xzf app.tar.gz -C prod-site
          cat prod-site/dist/index.html

# One-sentence explanation:
# Removing the 'environment: production' block (or removing the required-reviewer
# rule from that GitHub environment) eliminates the only pause point in the pipeline,
# so every commit that passes staging verification reaches production automatically.

# Part B — Recommendation:
# Recommended approach: start with Continuous Delivery (Engineer B is right for now).
#
# The team currently deploys manually once a week; their test suite has not been
# battle-tested in an automated CD context, and they have no baseline on rollback
# speed. Continuous Deployment requires deep confidence that automated checks catch
# every production-breaking change — confidence earned over time, not assumed.
#
# Before removing the approval gate they should verify: (1) test coverage is high
# enough that a green pipeline reliably means 'safe to ship', (2) they have fast
# automated rollback or a Blue-Green / Canary setup so a bad release can be
# reversed before most users are affected, and (3) lead time from commit to
# staging-verified is short (ideally under 15 minutes) so the human reviewer
# is not a bottleneck. Using feature toggles in the meantime lets them deploy
# code to production while keeping new features dark, reducing release risk
# without needing to block the pipeline.
tasks.explanation:
Part A insight: the `environment:` block in GitHub Actions is the entire mechanism that creates the Continuous Delivery gate. When GitHub sees a job targeting an environment that has required reviewers configured, it holds the job in 'waiting' state and notifies reviewers. Removing the block (or clearing the protection rules in the repository settings) means the job starts immediately after its `needs` dependency completes — that is exactly Continuous Deployment.

Part B insight: the lecture explicitly states that most teams adopt Continuous Delivery first and graduate to Continuous Deployment only once their test coverage and monitoring 'earn that trust'. The key conditions are: high automated test coverage (so the green pipeline is genuinely trustworthy), fast rollback capability (Blue-Green or Canary patterns), and short lead time so the human approval is a deliberate business decision rather than a bottleneck. Feature toggles further lower risk by decoupling the technical deploy event from the business release decision.
hints: For Part A, focus only on the `deploy-production` job. Everything else in the workflow stays the same. The difference between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment in GitHub Actions is literally the presence or absence of the `environment:` key (or the protection rules attached to that environment).

For Part B, consider the risk profile of the team. They currently deploy weekly, which means their batch sizes are large and their test suite may not be exercised at CD cadence. What would need to change for automated tests alone to be the safety net? The lecture mentions Flickr's example and the 'small batches are safer' principle — think about why.

Remember that 'deploy' and 'release' can be separated with feature toggles. This is relevant to Part B: even under Continuous Deployment, a feature toggle means a commit reaching production does not necessarily mean users see the new feature immediately, which reduces the stakes of removing the approval gate.
